name: Dorothy Donegan
abstract: Dorothy Donegan (April 6, 1922 – May 19, 1998) was an American classically trained jazz pianist and occasional vocalist, primarily known for performing stride and boogie-woogie, as well as bebop, swing, and classical.

interviews.title: Dorothy Donegan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: Schlitz Salute to Café Society

What Are You Doing the Rest of Your Life?

concerts.label: Kool Jazz Festival: Women Blow Their Own Horns

quotes.text: Yes it did. Because I hadn’t played Carnegie Hall. I wanted to play on that with Thelma Carpenter, she really didn’t want me on the bill, but I was hired.
quotes.caption: Donegan recalls performing with Thelma Carpenter at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Dorothy Donegan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Helen Humes
about_quotes.evidence: I have a friend and this girl is the best piano player in the whole world, Dorothy Donegan.

about_quotes.speaker: Sammy Price
about_quotes.evidence: And to hear him play with Dorothy Donegan was...she was fantastic and so was he, so...I think that you'd consider that... I'd consider it and classify it as a game of skill, have the two of them playing together, you know, as they did.

about_quotes.speaker: Marian McPartland
about_quotes.evidence: She did a great show, but she was so eager to outplay me that in the middle of the show I started to get a headache... At one point I played a tune, and she said, 'I really like that tune. I’d like to play you my version of it.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Marian McPartland — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Percy Gabriel
about_quotes.evidence: It was Duke Ellington, Dorothy Donegan, Art Tatum, and Jay McShann, Coleman Hawkins. Charlie Parker was playing alto. And Paul Quinichet on tenor. That session happened to be held in the Down Beat.

about_quotes.speaker: Jane Jarvis
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWEE] Dorothy Donegan. And there are plenty of fine women musicians out there that lose heart because there’s no place to go.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jane Jarvis interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie Williams
about_quotes.evidence: Eugene Smith... used to idolize Dorothy Donegan because she was such a great performer. And he was very fond of Dorothy’s playing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jackie Williams interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995
